paragraphs: Astronomy is often overlooked as a valuable resource for students of other scientific disciplines. In reality, a basic understanding of astronomical concepts can provide significant benefits and insights across various fields of study. In this volume, I intend to present an account of some attempts I have made in my leisure time over the past three years to explore whether any ideas could be gleaned about the early astronomical views of the Egyptians through a study of their temples and the mythology associated with various cults. It is possible to reconstruct my interest in this matter by examining the following statement: In March 1890, during a short break, I visited the Levant.

The curious alignment of the Parthenon's construction and the numerous directional shifts in its foundations at Eleusis, as revealed by French excavations, were striking and suggestive. I thought it worth noting these bearings to determine if there was any possible astronomical origin for the temple's direction and the various changes in direction that have been referred to. It is likely that astronomers from both France and Germany were aware of the potential astronomical significance of ancient temple construction. However, they may not have explicitly acknowledged or documented this knowledge. Nevertheless, it is clear that the orientation of these temples was influenced by astronomical considerations.

One individual, Brugsch Bey, showed great interest in the matter. He took the time to examine old inscriptions and eventually shared with me an intriguing discovery related to the temple's foundation at Edfu. I have thought it right to share this personal narrative, as it clarifies the connection between my work and Professor Nissen's. This allows me to express my gratitude for acknowledging that the initial idea originated solely with him, as far as I am aware. The determination of the stars to which some Egyptian temples, dedicated to a known deity, were aligned, revealed a pathway, as I had anticipated, to a study of the astronomical foundation underlying certain aspects of the mythology.

I have spent the last two years exploring the ground as far as my knowledge would allow, and I have come to the impression that both gods and goddesses will likely be found to be relatively few in number; that the apparent wealth of mythology is largely due to the totemism of the inhabitants in the Nile valley. This refers to the practice where each district had its own unique animal emblem representing the tribe living there, and every mythological figure was connected to these local cults in some way. It will be possible to begin answering some of the questions I have raised. In ancient times, the calendar underwent significant changes. The earliest known version was created by the Sumerians around 2100 BC, but it was later revised and adopted by other civilizations. The Babylonians, in particular, made notable contributions to the development of the calendar. They introduced the concept of a lunisolar calendar, which combined elements of both lunar and solar cycles. The ancient Egyptians also played a significant role in refining the calendar. They added months to the existing system and created a 365-day year. The Julian calendar, introduced by Julius Caesar in 45 BC, was another major revision. It was based on a solar year and introduced the concept of leap years. The Gregorian calendar, introduced in 1582 AD, is the most widely used calendar today. It refined the Julian calendar's system and eliminated errors that had accumulated over time. Throughout history, these revisions have shaped the modern calendar we use today.
